title: Processes, Signals, Jobs, and Resource Control · System Forensics
summary: Diagnose a worker that appears hung while preserving evidence, avoiding collateral termination, and distinguishing shell jobs from system processes. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case pr…
objectives: Relate executable files, processes, threads, parentage, sessions, process groups, state, and open resources.
Use ps, pgrep, pidof, top-like evidence, and proc data with filters that identify the intended process uniquely.
Send signals from least disruptive to forceful while accounting for handlers, permissions, descendants, and cleanup.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Inspect the failure record
steps.instruction:
debug case — Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2203; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup.

Inspect the Process Model trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for relate executable files, processes, threads, parentage, sessions, process groups, state, and open resources.

content.text: For Process Model, distinguish a claim from evidence by locating the relevant precondition, trace or invariant, and changed input result.

hints: Name the Process Model precondition or invariant that the debug case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2203; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: A program file and a running process are the same object.

stimulus.title: Process Model case record
stimulus.caption: Three excerpts from a learner evidence packet; only one connects a claim to a reproducible check.

lines.label: assumption
lines.text: Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2203; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup.

lines.label: risk
lines.text: A program file and a running process are the same object.

lines.label: evidence
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Relate executable files, processes, threads, parentage, sessions, process groups, state, and open resources. Artifact under review: a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook.

options.text: The record says the result looks right but supplies no quantity, trace, condition, or counterexample.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
The record names the assumption, applies Process Model, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.

steps.title: Priority Limits · fault isolation
steps.instruction:
debug case — Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2203; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup.

Which observation would disprove this suspected cause: “A negative nice value means a process uses fewer resources.”?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for interpret nice values, scheduling priority, resource limits, and pressure evidence without equating priority with guaranteed resources.

steps.title: Implement the cause-level repair
steps.instruction: Append a safe Linux operations investigation headed "# Evidence: linux-priority-limits-2yr4e2" with a concrete hypothesis, one bounded simulator command, and an observation that changes the next decision.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Priority Limits into observable performance evidence.

# hypothesis: [replace with one falsifiable cause]
# command: choose one bounded simulator observation
# observation: [replace with the result that changes the next decision]

options.text: Capture user, directory, environment, target state, and authoritative command behavior
Predict shell parsing, expansion, permissions, side effects, exit status, and safety boundary
Run the smallest simulated observation or reversible change and retain exact output
Verify changed state, failure path, rollback, and an accessible operations handoff

steps.title: Publish the incident record
steps.instruction: Write the debug evidence note for Priority Limits: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2206; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Interpret nice values, scheduling priority, resource limits, and pressure evidence without equating priority with guaranteed resources.
